title: Imputation: Filling the Holes
concept: Imputation is the process of filling in missing data values using statistical estimates like the mean, median, or a constant.

options.text: Data containing missing values, formatting inconsistencies, or duplicate entries
A dataset stored on a physically dirty hard drive
Information that has been encrypted for security

exercises.explain: Right! Dirty data has errors, duplicates, or missing spots that make it unusable for machine learning without cleaning.

cards.title: Mind the gap
cards.body: You have a customer list, but some users left their age blank. Most AI algorithms crash if they hit a blank space. What do you do?

cards.title: What is Imputation?
cards.body: Imputation means filling in missing values with smart guesses, rather than throwing the data away.

cards.title: Pasting missing puzzle pieces
cards.body: Imagine a jigsaw puzzle with a few missing sky pieces. Instead of throwing the puzzle away, you paint matching blue shapes to fill the holes.

cards.title: Mean, median, and constants
cards.body: If customer ages are missing, you could fill them with the average (mean) age, the middle value (median), or a constant default like 99.

cards.title: Filled data isn't real data
cards.body: Imputation is a helper, but it doesn't add real information. Over-using it can trick your model into seeing patterns that aren't actually there.

cards.title: Mean vs Constant Imputation
cards.body: Two common ways to fill missing numbers:

left.text:
Fills holes with average
Keeps overall average same
Can mask extreme variations

right.text:
Fills holes with a fixed value
Like filling missing with '0'
Creates a spike at that value

options.text: Filling in missing values with estimated numbers or categories
Deleting all columns that have empty spaces
Encrypting data so it cannot be stolen
Multiplying the data by the number of columns

exercises.explain: Yes! Imputation keeps your dataset intact by filling holes with statistics like the mean or median.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Filling missing values with the average (mean) is always perfectly accurate and has no risks.
exercises.explain: Correct! Imputation is always an estimate. Filling too many blanks with the mean can reduce variability and hide real patterns.

exercises.sentence: Imputation keeps your dataset whole by ___ missing values.
exercises.explain: Exactly. It fills in the gaps so algorithms can run without throwing errors.

exercises.prompt: Which one is an example of median imputation?

options.text: Replacing a missing age with the middle value of all known ages
Deleting every customer record that has a missing age
Replacing a missing age with the number 999

exercises.explain: Spot on! The median is the middle number when sorted, making it robust against extreme outliers.

exercises.prompt: You're predicting salaries and notice a few blanks in the 'Years of Experience' column. Most salaries are $50k-$80k, but one executive makes $2 Million. Which statistical filler is safest from this massive outlier?

options.text: Median imputation, because it ignores extreme outliers
Mean imputation, because it averages everything together
Zero imputation, to assume they have no experience

exercises.explain: Brilliant! The mean would be dragged up by the executive's huge salary, but the median (middle value) stays safe from extreme outliers. You're ready to learn when NOT to fill!
